attributes.stem:
A 64-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of progressively worsening right buttock pain that comes on when he walks approximately 100 metres on a flat surface. The walking distance shortens if he is on an incline. The pain radiates down the right thigh and usually disappears when he rests, although occasionally occurs sporadically at rest nocturnally. He also suffers from ongoing back pain that he attributes to his lifetime as a builder, though he has recently retired. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, erectile dysfunction and hypercholesterolaemia. His regular medications include metformin, perindopril and atorvastatin. He recently quit smoking on the background of a 45 pack-year history and drinks alcohol on weekends (around 15 standard drinks).

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The lower limbs are relatively pale with the right leg being cooler to touch than the left. Only bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses are palpable. The right femoral pulse is relatively diminished compared to the left. He has normal movements of all lower limb joints on passive and active movement, with normal power (5/5). Neurological examination of the right leg identifies sensory deficits to light touch and pain distally that returns as the examination moves proximally. Straight leg raise is negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Stenosis of the right common iliac artery
attributes.explanation:
This man has a significant number of cardiovascular risk factors that dramatically increase his chances of developing atherosclerotic insults, such as stenosis of the right common iliac artery. These include smoking and alcohol consumption, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, and type 2 diabetes. The symptoms are classic of ischaemic pain caused by intermittent claudication of the larger muscles of the lower limb. As the stenosis has occurred in the common iliac artery, there are symptoms evident in the muscles supplied by the external iliac artery (buttocks and thigh) as well as the internal iliac artery (pudendal artery) causing erectile dysfunction. The stem also outlines that there is sporadic discomfort at rest, indicating a severe level of stenosis of the artery.

Spinal canal stenosis is an important differential diagnosis. This is caused most commonly by degenerative changes to the intervertebral discs, causing circumferential disc bulging into the spinal canal. Risk factors for spinal canal stenosis include ageing, manual labour, previous injury, smoking and diabetes, and it is often accompanied by activity-related back pain, weakness and sensory changes to the lower limbs. However, due to the high number of cardiovascular risk factors present, the negative straight leg raising test and reduction in peripheral pulses, this is less likely to be the diagnosis.

L4,5 disc compression is less likely in this case as the pattern of motor weakness and/or sensory distribution is specific to the region of the spine involved. If L4,5 were involved in this case, the sensory deficits would be experienced in the L4,5 dermatomes (lateral calf, dorsum of foot, web space between first and second toe). The motor deficits would also be specific for L4,5 spinal level (hip abduction, knee flexion, foot dorsiflexion, toe extension and flexion, foot inversion and eversion).

Right-sided sciatica typically presents with pain radiating from the lower back down the posterior aspect of the leg, often accompanied by numbness or tingling. While this patient does have pain radiating down the right thigh, the primary symptom of buttock pain with walking is more consistent with vascular claudication. Additionally, the negative straight leg raise test and the presence of cardiovascular risk factors make sciatica less likely.

Right-sided acute limb ischaemia is characterized by the sudden onset of severe pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, and paralysis (the 6 P's). This patient's symptoms have been progressive over two months and worsen with exertion, which is more consistent with chronic ischaemia due to arterial stenosis rather than an acute event. The presence of palpable femoral and popliteal pulses, albeit diminished on the right, also argues against acute limb ischaemia.

In conclusion, the patient's cardiovascular risk factors, classic symptoms of intermittent claudication, and physical examination findings (including diminished pulses and cooler right leg) strongly support the diagnosis of stenosis of the right common iliac artery as the most likely cause of his symptoms.
learningPoints: Atherosclerotic stenosis of the common iliac artery results in symptoms evident in the muscles supplied by the external iliac artery (buttocks and thigh) as well as the internal iliac artery (pudendal artery) which can cause erectile dysfunction.
references: Mahé G, Kaladji A, Le Faucheur A, Jaquinandi V. Internal Iliac Artery Stenosis: Diagnosis and How to Manage it in 2015. Front Cardiovasc Med. 2015;2:33. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4671337/
Paoloni J, MSpMed FA. Approach to the adult with unspecified hip pain. In: UpToDate [online]. 2020. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/approach-to-the-adult-with-unspecified-hip-pain
